text: In this image you can see the completely freed abdominal oesophagus emerging from the triangular shape hiatus.
This image shows the space where you must perform blunt careful finger dissection freeing the esophagus right around in a caudal to cranial fashion.
The transthoracic mobilization is done via a posterolateral thoracotomy or via a thoracoscopic axis.
In this image, such a para-esophageal node is shown by a white arrow just right to the rubber sling lifting the esophagus up.
This image shows the close proximity of the thoracic duct to the oesophagus which is being pulled down.
After neoadjuvant chemo irradiation, the thoracic duct may be completely adhered to the oesophagus.
Therefore, clear visualization is needed to prevent injury.

After clearing the nodes around the carina, a supra-aceitis mobilization is carried out, separating the oesophagus from the posterior surface of the trachea, marked here with a T. The trachealis muscle is a thin structure, therefore it is advisable to use the harmonic scalpel instead of the diatomy to prevent lateral thermal injury.
Also note the white arrow here pointing to the right recurrent laryngeal nerve.
